skill.name: python-best-practices
skill.description: Provides Python patterns for type-first development with dataclasses, discriminated unions, NewType, and Protocol. Must use when reading or writing Python files.
skill.summary: Provides Python patterns for type-first development with dataclasses, discriminated unions, NewType,...
skill.author: unknown

security_audit.summary: This skill has low-risk features: env_access, network. No dangerous patterns detected.

content.value_statement: This skill provides a comprehensive guide to type-first development in Python, focusing on creating robust, maintainable code through advanced type hints. By utilizing patterns like dataclasses, discriminated unions, NewType, and Protocols, developers can define strict contracts that prevent invalid states at compile-time. This approach moves error detection from runtime to development, significantly reducing bugs related to data mutation and incorrect type usage. It is essential for teams looking to scale Python applications while ensuring code reliability and clarity.
actual_capabilities: Define immutable data structures using frozen dataclasses
Implement discriminated unions for state management with Literal types
Enforce domain-specific type safety using NewType
Define structural subtyping contracts using Protocol
Model external JSON data shapes accurately with TypedDict
Apply functional patterns to avoid mutable state
limitations: Requires a static type checker (like mypy or pyright) to enforce constraints effectively
Adds initial boilerplate code for defining types and classes
Runtime type validation is not automatic for TypedDict or Protocol and requires manual implementation
May introduce a learning curve for developers accustomed to dynamic typing

use_cases.title: Robust API Data Modeling
use_cases.description: Use dataclasses and TypedDict to strictly model request and response payloads, ensuring data integrity at system boundaries.

use_cases.title: Flexible Plugin Architecture
use_cases.description: Utilize Protocol to define interfaces for plugins, allowing users to implement classes without explicit inheritance.

use_cases.title: State Machine Management
use_cases.description: Implement discriminated unions to handle complex state transitions (e.g., Idle, Loading, Success, Error) safely.

prompt_templates.title: Create Immutable Domain Model
prompt_templates.scenario: Defining a core entity like a User or Order
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a frozen dataclass named 'Invoice' with fields for 'id' (str), 'amount' (Decimal), and 'status' (Literal['paid', 'unpaid']).

prompt_templates.title: Refactor to NewType
prompt_templates.scenario: Preventing mixing of similar string types
prompt_templates.prompt: Refactor the function signature 'process_user(user_id: str)' to use NewType to ensure 'UserId' cannot be confused with a standard string or 'OrderId'.

prompt_templates.title: Implement Error Handling
prompt_templates.scenario: Catching and re-raising exceptions with context
prompt_templates.prompt: Write a try-except block that attempts to parse JSON data and re-raises the exception with a custom message while preserving the original traceback.

output_examples.input: Define a discriminated union for a Result type

best_practices: Use @dataclass(frozen=True) to ensure immutability and prevent accidental state mutations.
Define types and function signatures before writing implementation logic.
Use discriminated unions (Literal types) to make illegal states unrepresentable.
Propagate exceptions using 'raise ... from err' to preserve stack traces.
Prefer Protocol over inheritance for defining shared behaviors (structural typing).
anti_patterns: Using mutable default arguments in function definitions (e.g., def foo(x=[])).
Relying solely on runtime logic to enforce type constraints that could be caught by static checkers.
Swallowing exceptions silently without logging or re-raising them.
Using class-level mutable state which leads to side effects.
Creating monolithic modules; prefer splitting files by concern.

faq.question: Why should I use NewType instead of just str?
faq.answer: NewType creates a distinct type at static type-checking time. This prevents you from accidentally passing a UserId where an OrderId is expected, even though both are strings at runtime.

faq.question: Do frozen dataclasses impact performance?
faq.answer: The impact is negligible. The safety gained from immutability (preventing bugs) far outweighs the minimal overhead of creating a new instance when data needs to change.

faq.question: When should I use Protocol instead of an Abstract Base Class (ABC)?
faq.answer: Use Protocol when you care about the structure (methods/attributes) of an object rather than its inheritance hierarchy. It allows classes to implement the protocol without explicitly inheriting from it.
